eli3: The chain rule tells you how to differentiate composed functions. If y = f(g(x)), the rate of change of y with respect to x is the product: dy/dx = f'(g(x)) · g'(x). Think of it as rates multiplying along a chain.

Imagine a production line: machine A processes input x into intermediate g(x), then machine B processes that into final output f(g(x)). The chain rule says: total change = (B's sensitivity to input) × (A's sensitivity to input).

In multiple dimensions, the chain rule uses matrix multiplication (Jacobians). If h(x) = f(g(x)), then Jh = Jf · Jg. This is why backpropagation works — gradients flow backward through the network via successive matrix multiplications.

whenWhy.title: Backpropagation in neural networks
whenWhy.body: Neural networks are compositions of layers: fₙ∘fₙ₋₁∘…∘f₁(x). Backprop applies chain rule backward from loss to compute ∂L/∂W for all weights. Each layer passes gradients to previous layer via Jacobian-vector products.

whenWhy.title: Change of variables in probability
whenWhy.body: For random variable transformation Y = g(X), the PDF transforms as f_Y(y) = f_X(g⁻¹(y)) · |d/dy g⁻¹(y)|. The determinant of the Jacobian accounts for how g stretches/compresses space.

whenWhy.title: Differentiating complex loss functions
whenWhy.body: Loss functions often compose multiple operations: L = loss(model(parameters(data))). Chain rule decomposes this into manageable pieces: ∂L/∂params = ∂L/∂output × ∂output/∂params.

whenWhy.title: ODEs and dynamical systems
whenWhy.body: If y depends on x, and x depends on t, then dy/dt = (dy/dx)(dx/dt). Used extensively in physics (related rates), control theory, and when analyzing optimization dynamics (gradient flow).

deepDiveIntro: For y = f(g(x)) where f: ℝ → ℝ and g: ℝ → ℝ, the chain rule states dy/dx = f'(g(x)) · g'(x). In Leibniz notation: dy/dx = (dy/dg)(dg/dx). This extends to multiple variables: if z = f(x(t), y(t)), then dz/dt = (∂z/∂x)(dx/dt) + (∂z/∂y)(dy/dt).

For vector functions, if h(x) = f(g(x)) where g: ℝⁿ → ℝᵐ and f: ℝᵐ → ℝᵖ, then Jh(x) = Jf(g(x)) · Jg(x) — matrix multiplication of Jacobians. The (i,j) entry is Σₖ (∂fᵢ/∂yₖ)(∂yₖ/∂xⱼ). This form is fundamental to automatic differentiation and backpropagation.

deepDive.heading: Single Variable Chain Rule
deepDive.body: For composition f∘g: (f∘g)'(x) = f'(g(x)) · g'(x). Intuition: change in f due to change in g, times change in g due to change in x. Leibniz notation makes it look like fraction cancellation: dy/dx = (dy/dg)(dg/dx).

deepDive.heading: Multivariable Chain Rule
deepDive.body: For z = f(x(t), y(t)): dz/dt = (∂f/∂x)(dx/dt) + (∂f/∂y)(dy/dt). General form: if x depends on t, then df/dt = ∇fᵀ (dx/dt). Each path from t to f contributes a product of partials; sum over all paths.

deepDive.heading: Chain Rule for Jacobians
deepDive.body: For h(x) = f(g(x)) with g: ℝⁿ → ℝᵐ, f: ℝᵐ → ℝᵖ: Jh(x) = Jf(g(x)) · Jg(x) ∈ ℝᵖˣⁿ. Matrix multiplication corresponds to composing linear approximations. This is the mathematical foundation of backpropagation.

deepDive.heading: Backpropagation as Chain Rule
deepDive.body: For neural network L = fₙ∘…∘f₁(x), the gradient ∂L/∂Wₖ requires Jacobian of all layers after k. Backprop efficiently computes this via vector-Jacobian products (VJPs) passing backward: vᵀJₙ, then (vᵀJₙ)Jₙ₋₁, etc. Complexity is linear in network depth.

deepDive.heading: Higher-Order Chain Rule (Faà di Bruno)
deepDive.body: Generalizes to higher derivatives. The formula involves Bell polynomials and sums over partitions. Rarely used directly in ML, but relevant for higher-order optimization methods (Newton, natural gradient) and analyzing curvature of composed functions.

takeaways: Multivariable: dz/dt = Σ (∂f/∂xᵢ)(dxᵢ/dt). Sum over all paths.
Vector version: Jh = Jf · Jg (matrix multiplication of Jacobians).
Backpropagation is efficient application of chain rule via VJPs.
Leibniz notation makes chain rule look like fraction cancellation.

edgeCases.q: What if g is not differentiable at some point?
edgeCases.a: The chain rule requires differentiability. If g is not differentiable at x but f is constant near g(x), f∘g might still be differentiable. For non-differentiable g (like ReLU at 0), we use subgradients: ∂(f∘g)(x) contains products of subgradients.

edgeCases.q: How does chain rule work with higher-order derivatives?
edgeCases.a: Apply product rule to chain rule result. For (f∘g)'': differentiate f'(g(x))g'(x) using both product and chain rules: (f∘g)'' = f''(g)(g')² + f'(g)g''. This gets complicated quickly (Faà di Bruno formula).

edgeCases.q: Why is backpropagation more efficient than naive chain rule?
edgeCases.a: Naive evaluation would compute Jacobians explicitly (large matrices). Backprop uses vector-Jacobian products (VJPs), computing vᵀJ without forming J. This reduces complexity from O(nodes²) to O(nodes) for typical network architectures.

sources.label: Stewart — Calculus: Early Transcendentals (8th ed.), Ch. 3
Baydin et al. — Automatic Differentiation in Machine Learning
